story:
Dolly Jameson, fifty-two, single, and disciplined to the minute, lived by a clock: 6:15 a.m., prayer, tea, gospel hum, work. ZoZo—fat, grey, and magnificently judgmental—watched from the stairs like a supervisor with a tail. Then one Tuesday, routine snapped. Her alarm failed. She woke at 7:02. ZoZo took the stairs one paw at a time as if time were infinite; Dolly tumbled past, missed the bus, and stepped into a rainstorm that baptized her five times in filthy street water. “You look baptized,” an old man said, not unkindly. At the office, squelching and smeared, she learned she was first up to present the quarterly report she’d assembled. In the restroom, her reflection lagged, then smiled without her. Dolly shut her eyes—hard.

She woke to the right sound at the right time: 6:15 a.m. Alarm cheerful. ZoZo licking a paw at the foot of the bed. Safe. Then, on the stairs, ZoZo spoke. “Don’t miss your bus, Dolly.” Her teacup shattered. She pinched herself; it hurt. Outside, the rain and the five splashes returned in the same cruel rhythm. The old man reappeared. “You again?” he asked gently. At work, she went straight to the restroom mirror. The reflection spoke. “I’m you,” it said. “Or you’re me.” “I just want to wake up,” Dolly whispered. “Then close your eyes.”

She did, and woke—again—at 6:15. Only the clock flickered between 6:15 and 7:02, then scrambled into nonsense. Downstairs, a pot simmered; the rice smelled burnt. The date on her phone had slipped to Wednesday. “Time is flexible when you don’t believe in it,” ZoZo said, infuriating and calm. Mr. Banda called: the shareholders were waiting. Dolly blinked and stood in front of a projector, throat full of cotton, as the room turned toward the window in eerie unison. Outside, the bus sprayed the glass. ZoZo’s voice floated in: “You missed the bus again, Dolly.” She screamed and fell through the dark.

A hospital bed. Tubes. A nurse fluffing blankets. “You’ve been asleep for two days, Ms. Jameson.” Relief—until she read the nurse’s name tag: ZoZo. Rain tapped the window. Two weeks later, Dolly rebuilt her routine, told herself it had been a stress dream. Sometimes, though, she heard ZoZo whisper “Again.” She told herself she imagined it.

The morning her alarm returned to its faithful 6:15, she smiled—until she saw ZoZo on the stairs, moving deliberately, one paw at a time. The clock flipped to 7:02. Rain began to fall inside her bedroom. Splash. Water hit her face even though she stood indoors. “You’re late, Dolly,” ZoZo said.

Dolly laughed, hysterical and hollow. Downstairs, the house had become a hall of mirrors. In every one, a different Dolly moved out of sync: one crying, one brushing her hair, one delivering the presentation with perfect poise. “Which one is real?” she asked. “All of them,” ZoZo said. “None of them. You decide.” “I want to wake up.” “Then sleep.” “And if I’m asleep?” “Then dream better.”

Later—if “later” existed—her neighbor knocked. No answer. The landlord unlocked the door and opened the spotless house. A teacup on the table. Lunch neatly packed on the counter. An alarm blinking 6:15. ZoZo sat on the stairs, eyes bright, tail ticking time. The landlord paused. “She finally caught the bus,” he said, soft and almost amused, as rain began again.
